3A_Bibliographic.citation: Guerrero, M. D., Barnes, J. D., Chaput, J.-P., & Tremblay, M. S. (2019). Screen time and problem behaviors in children: Exploring the mediating role of sleep duration. International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity, 16, 105.
3A_Bibliographic.journal: International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We used baseline (cross-sectional) data (2016-18) from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study, a US representative sample of 11,875 children aged 9 to 10 years.
3B_Sample_Data.notes: Full ABCD baseline sample; varying Ns across measures (see Table 1)

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Screen time by type (TV/movies, videos, video games, social media) and screen content (mature-rated video games, R-rated movies)
3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey (14 items)
3C_Exposure.modalities: TV/movies, videos, video games, social media (text + social networking + video chat), mature-rated video games, R-rated movies
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Categorical (4 groups: <1h, 1-1.99h, 2-2.99h, >=3h per day for types; 4-point scale for content)
3C_Exposure.reporter: Self-report (youth) for screen time; parent-report for sleep duration and problem behaviors
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Recreational ST was measured using the Youth Screen Time Survey (14 items). Children were asked to report the number of hours spent on a typical weekday and weekend day for the following six ST types... Daily recreational ST for all six behaviors was calculated by taking a weighted average of the weekday and weekend ST activity... ST behaviors were then categorized into four different groups: <1h, 1-1.99h, 2-2.99h, and >=3h for analysis.
3C_Exposure.notes: Social media was collapsed from texting + social networking + video chat due to low use

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Problem behaviors (8 CBCL syndrome scales) and sleep duration
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) - 8 syndrome scales: anxious/depressed, withdrawn/depressed, somatic complaints, social problems, thought problems, attention problems, rule-breaking behavior, aggressive behavior; Parent Sleep Disturbance Scale (1 item for sleep duration)
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: The Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) is a parent-report measure used to assess a broad range of emotional and behavioral syndromes among children aged 6 to 18 years. The CBCL comprises eight syndrome scales: anxious/depressed, withdrawn/depressed, somatic complaints, social problems, thought problems, attention problems, rule-breaking behavior, and aggressive behavior.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Negative binomial structural equation model (SEM) with maximum likelihood estimation, robust standard errors, TYPE=COMPLEX for clustering; Mplus 8.2; mediation via MODEL INDIRECT with bootstrapping (B=5000)
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: sex, parental education, family income, ethnicity, physical activity, body mass index
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: The CBCL syndrome scales were treated as count variables. Because the CBCL syndrome scales displayed overdispersion, we applied a negative binomial distribution... Using the MODEL INDIRECT command in Mplus, we examined the mediating role of sleep duration by testing all possible indirect pathways between each ST behavior (type and content) and behavioral syndrome. Bootstrapping was used to test the significance of the mediation/indirect effects.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Unstandardized B from negative binomial model; IRR=1.059 (5.9% increase per category)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 2: Television/movies -> Rule-breaking behavior: B=.06, p<.001, 95% CI .025, .090, IRR=1.059, 5.9% change
Table 2: Television/movies -> Social problems: B=.05, p<.001, 95% CI .024, .073, IRR=1.050, 5%
Table 2: Television/movies -> Aggressive behavior: B=.04, p=.001, 95% CI .015, .063, IRR=1.040, 4%
Table 2: Video games -> Withdrawn/depressed: B=.04, p=.034, 95% CI .002, .060, IRR=1.031, 3.1%

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Unstandardized B; IRR=0.834 (16.6% decrease per hour of sleep)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 2: Sleep duration -> Thought problems: B=-.18, p<.001, 95% CI -.202, -.161, IRR=.834, -16.6%
Table 2: Mature-rated video games -> Somatic complaints: B=.04, p=.009, 95% CI .010, .071, IRR=1.041, 4.1%
Table 2: Social media -> Rule-breaking behavior: B=.08, p<.001, 95% CI .046, .115, IRR=1.084, 8.4%

3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: Sleep duration emerged as a significant mediator, though the effect sizes were small... The indirect effects of ST behaviors were small (see Table 3)... Given that the effect sizes of sleep were very modest, other potential sleep-related mediating variables need to be investigated in future research.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper consistently acknowledges small effect sizes and does not override this with accumulation arguments.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Parents can help establish healthy screen media habits for children that can facilitate sleep by speaking with their children about the importance of sleep, developing a bedtime that allows for adequate sleep, encouraging children to engage in calming activities in the evening rather than using electronic devices, and developing a predictable bedtime routine.
3H_Recommendations.notes: Recommendations are general sleep hygiene advice, less specifically tied to the small screen time effects

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion finds that most direct effects of screen time types on problem behaviors were not significant, with some exceptions for TV/movies, video games, and social media on specific syndromes. Sleep duration emerged as a stronger and more consistent predictor of all problem behaviors than screen time. The paper frames sleep as a partial mediator but notes small indirect effects. It acknowledges cross-sectional limitations and calls for longitudinal research. Recommendations focus on sleep hygiene rather than screen time reduction per se. Overall, a relatively measured interpretation.
